abstract: Speech brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) offer a transformative pathway for restoring communication to individuals with paralysis by translating neural signals associated with attempted speech into text or synthesized sound. While early iterations showed promise, they struggled to maintain accuracy when decoding unconstrained, large-vocabulary sentences. In this study, we present a high-performance speech-to-text BCI utilizing intracortical microelectrode arrays to record single-neuron activity. Our participant, who lost the ability to speak intelligibly due to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), achieved a 9.1% word error rate on a 50-word vocabulary—a nearly three-fold improvement over previous benchmarks. More notably, we achieved a 23.8% word error rate on a 125,000-word vocabulary, marking what we believe is the first successful demonstration of large-vocabulary decoding. The system reached a decoding speed of 62 words per minute, surpassing the prior record by a factor of 3.4 and moving closer to the pace of natural conversation. Our findings further reveal two critical properties of the neural speech code: the spatial intermixing of tuning for speech articulators, which allows for high-fidelity decoding from small cortical areas, and the long-term preservation of detailed phonetic representations years after the onset of paralysis. Collectively, these advancements demonstrate a viable clinical path toward restoring rapid, fluent communication for those who have lost the power of speech.
